coredata.dc:title: Lanthanum nitrogen Co-doped carbon quantum dots as optical and smartphone sensors for serotonin detection
coredata.dc:creator: Duhan, Jyoti
Obrai, Sangeeta
coredata.dc:description: A simple bottom up approach using hydrothermal technique was applied to prepare lanthanum and nitrogen Co-doped carbon quantum dots and were characterized by XRD, EDS, TEM, SEM FT-IR, XPS, fluorescence and UV-VIS spectroscopy to study their crystal structure, elemental and chemical composition, particle size, morphology and optical properties. It was found that morphology was spherical and particle size was 3–4 nm. EDS and XPS helps to prove the successful doping of lanthanum and nitrogen to carbon quantum dots with excitation and emission wavelengths 340 and 445 nm respectively. The intense blue color of fluorescent QDs were quenched with addition of serotonin due to the electrostatic interactions, π-π stacking interaction and may be due to complex formation between La,N-CQDs/Ser and the static quenching mechanism with inner filter effect has been observed. These La,N-CQDs were used as fluorescent probes for sensitive and selective detection of serotonin in a good linear range of 0–38 nM with low limit of detection 7.4 nM.The smartphone based portable detection was also developed for detection of serotonin providing the reliable information for analysis of color change and real sample analysis studies were also carried out. The uniqueness, high sensitivity and accuracy of the optical probe that was constructed are confirmed and this method's LOD is also highly competitive.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Serotonin
Static quenching
Inner filter effect
Smartphone sensors
Real sample analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Smartphone application for detection of Ser
Detection of Ser on colorimetric test papers

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of Human Resource and Development

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Dr B R Ambedkar National Institute of Technology Jalandhar

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: I want to express my gratitude to the Central Research Facility at NIT Jalandhar as well as the Ministry of Human Resource and Development (MHRD) of India for the financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) La,N-CQDs TEM images (b) average particle size of QDs (c) SEM image of synthesised QDs (d) XRD spectra and (e) FT-IR spectrum of La,N-CQDs. (f) Zeta-Potential plot of La,N-CQDs, Ser and mixture.

(a) Shows XPS full scan spectrum of La,N-CQD (b) C1s High resolution spectra (c) High resolution spectra of N1s (d) High resolution spectra of O1s (e) High resolution spectra of La(3d5/2) (f) High resolution spectra of La(3d3/2).
Shows the (a) absorbance and emission spectrum (b) PL intensity at various excitation wavelengths (c) Effect of pH on PL intensity (d) Histogram plots pH versus intensity.

(a) UV Vis absorption spectra of La,N-CQDs with sequential addition of Ser (b) spectral overlaps between absorbance spectra of Ser and excitation spectra of La,N-CQDs (c) Fluorescence spectra of La,N-CQDs with continuous addition of Ser (d) Stern-volmer plot for fluorescence quenching of La,N-CQDs with addition of Ser.
(a) Fluorescence lifetime decay curve of La,N-CQDs with and without Ser (b) smartphone detection of Ser with La,N-CQDs R/B values with conc.
Photos of NIT.J word written on two filter paper (a) under visible light (b) under UV lamp (c) when water sprayed on words (left) and Ser sprayed on paper under UV light.
(a) PL quenching efficiency of La,N-CQDs towards different species (b) Selectivity of La,N-CQDs towards serotonin.
Schematic illustration of synthesis La,N-CQD and quenching with Serotonin.
Lifetime measurements of La,N-CQD and La,N-CQD + Ser complex.
Real sample analysis of Serotonin in human urine samples.

head.ce:title: Lanthanum nitrogen Co-doped carbon quantum dots as optical and smartphone sensors for serotonin detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Dr. BR Ambedkar National Institute of Technology, Jalandhar, Punjab, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Dr. BR Ambedkar National Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Dr. BR Ambedkar National Institute of Technology Jalandhar, Punjab, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple bottom up approach using hydrothermal technique was applied to prepare lanthanum and nitrogen Co-doped carbon quantum dots and were characterized by XRD, EDS, TEM, SEM FT-IR, XPS, fluorescence and UV-VIS spectroscopy to study their crystal structure, elemental and chemical composition, particle size, morphology and optical properties. It was found that morphology was spherical and particle size was 3–4 nm. EDS and XPS helps to prove the successful doping of lanthanum and nitrogen to carbon quantum dots with excitation and emission wavelengths 340 and 445 nm respectively. The intense blue color of fluorescent QDs were quenched with addition of serotonin due to the electrostatic interactions, π-π stacking interaction and may be due to complex formation between La,N-CQDs/Ser and the static quenching mechanism with inner filter effect has been observed. These La,N-CQDs were used as fluorescent probes for sensitive and selective detection of serotonin in a good linear range of 0–38 nM with low limit of detection 7.4 nM.The smartphone based portable detection was also developed for detection of serotonin providing the reliable information for analysis of color change and real sample analysis studies were also carried out. The uniqueness, high sensitivity and accuracy of the optical probe that was constructed are confirmed and this method's LOD is also highly competitive.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Serotonin shows pleiotropic activity and belongs to alkyl indole groups [1,2]. Serotonin (Ser) hormone performs various functions in managing mood disorder, sleep wake cycle [3,4] obesity, depression, aging, migraines, unregulated haemostasis and blood clotting. Low levels of Ser in body cause several health disorders like breast cancer [5,6], prostate cancer, oxidative anxiety (can damage all components of cell like DNA, protein and lipids), parkinsonism and Alzheimer diseases [7,8], jet leg, moment disorders etc. On the other hand high level of this hormone in body may cause toxicity and potentially fatal effects [9,10]. Average amount of serotonin present in human body is 10 mg and its 2 % is found in the brain and central nervous system. Serotonin also known as 5- hydroxy tryptamine and all of these earlier findings have hinted at the possibility of using 5- hydroxy tryptamine(5-HT) as a biomarker in a variety of disorders. The creation of techniques to detect 5-HT is a topic of interest in this situation. Since last decades Ser investigation in biological and pharmaceutical samples continue by using various methods like High performance liquid Chromatography (HPLC) [11,12], gas Chromatography [13,14], capillary electrophoresis [15,16], radioimmunoassay (RIA) [17,18], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [19,20], mass spectrometry [21,22], ultra performance liquid chromatography (UPLC/MS-MS) and electrochemical techniques [23–30]. While the majority of these techniques can test 5-HT properly but these have various limitations such as expensive techniques, difficult to operate, low sensitivity, take more time and lacks of portability. Straightforward methods to find 5-HT is still difficult, nevertheless, because of the molecule's complexity.

Due to their potential for use in the field of biomedicine, functional nanomaterials have gained a lot of attention [31–36]. A class of semiconductor nanoparticles known as carbon quantum dots (CQDs) can be employed for photovoltaic devices, medication delivery, bioimaging, and disease detection [37–41]. Finding the best nanomaterials for biological markers, illness diagnosis, and medicine delivery is crucial. CQDs, the fluorescent carbon nanomaterials are relatively less toxic and biocompatible as carbon is particularly appealing as a component of all species skeletons [43]. Cells are capable of ingesting CQDs that enter the interior of the cell without harming the nucleus. Moreover, CQDs can interact with DNA indicating strong DNA sensitivity and selectivity [44,45]. Easily quenched in-solution by electron acceptor, CQDs are a novel class of fluorescent probe for both metal ions and biomolecules [46,47]. The metal-doped CQDs revealed better antibacterial activities, multicolour emission and unique functions relative to undoped and also have greater photoluminescence quantum yields [48–50]. For this reason, metal-doped fluorescent CQDs can be designed as serotonin sensors owing to their enhanced sensing capabilities. The literature suggests that lanthanum could enhance CQDs fluorescence and quantum yields. For instance, Yang et al. testified that the fluorescence intensity and quantum yields of CQDs were improved after doping the La into CQDs [51]. Zhang et al. reported that the fluorescence intensity of La doped graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets was improved compared with undoped graphitic carbon nitride nanosheet [52]. Due to their distinctive atomic structure, rare earth elements have unique features. The use of rare-earth nanoparticles in bioimaging is widespread likewise biosensing [53]. Furthermore, it has been demonstrated that lanthanide has a considerable impact on the photoluminescence property of inorganic nanocrystals [54]. In order to enhance the original property of doped CQDs it is therefore fair to predict that a rare earth element can function as an additional heteroatom during the creation of Co-doped CQDs [55].

This work describes the hydrothermal synthesis of multifunctional and photoluminescent La,N-CQDs from citric acid, urea and lanthanum oxides as shown in Scheme 1. The average size of blue luminescent La,N-CQDs was 3–4 nm with short fluorescence lifetime, good stability and high pH sensitivity. Surprisingly, the produced La,N-CQDs demonstrated excellent stability with time and can be utilised to identify serotonin in aqueous solutions. Structural characterisation with transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction, scanning electron microscope (SEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier transform infrared spectra and EDS proved the successful synthesis of La,N-CQDs. The sensitive and specific serotonin detection is proved with UV-VIS and PL spectra and the quenching mechanism has been evidenced with TCS-PC and zeta potential. Also, we developed smartphone portable sensor for the detection of serotonin using synthesised La,N-CQDs.

Serotonin and melatonin were bought from sigma Aldrich. Lanthanum oxides (La2O3), urea, citric acid, glucose, dopa, phenylalanine, lysine, glycine, alanine, HCl, NaOH pellets were of analytical grade purchased from loba. All chemicals were used directly without any refinement and distilled water was used for preparations of solutions.

UV–Vis absorption spectra were taken in UV-VIS spectrophotometer (Agilent), fluorescence spectra were taken using photoluminescence spectrophotometer with an excitation, emission wavelength of 340 and 450 nm respectively and FT-IR spectra was recorded in range of 500–4000 cm−1. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) detected the size of QDs using 300kv accelerating voltage, EDS was used for elemental analysis, scanning electron microscope (SEM) for morphology, X-Ray diffractions (XRD) for angle and X-Ray photoelectron spectroscopy for binding energy and chemical states, Dynamic light scattering for measuring zeta potential at 18 °C. RGB color detector software was used for detection of variation in color of QDs with addition of serotonin. Time resolved fluorescence spectrometer were used for finding lifetime and studying mechanism of interactions.

The La,N-CQDs were synthesised by using formerly reported methods with slight modifications using lanthanum oxides, urea and citric acid. La2O3 (0.36 g) and urea(0.32 g) were mixed in 5 ml distilled water(D.W) and then put on magnetic stirrer for 10 min so that a clear solution was formed. Citric acid (0.5 g) was dissolved in another beaker containing 5 ml D.W and put on ultrasonic bath. Then both the solutions were mixed on magnetic stirrer for 30 min and added to Teflon lined autoclave and first heated at 160 °C for 10 h and cooled down to room temperature in hydrothermal for 3 h shown in Scheme 1 . After cooling, the green solution was obtained and further dissolved in 10 ml D.W. The solution was filtered to eliminate impurities and then centrifugation at 10,000 rpm was carried out for 20min. The clear solution was put in dialysis membrane(1000Da) for 48 h to remove large particles and stored in freezer at 4 °C for further use.

Typically, La,N-CQDs/Ser complex was prepared by dilution of QDs till 10−6 M solutions and Ser solutions were made by using 30 μL of 30 % H2O2 and D.W at room temperature and mixture solutions were put for 10min to react and then fluorescence readings were taken. Optimization of synthesised QDs were noted by varying pH from 1 to 12 at different time and at various excitation wavelength. Selectivity study was observed with addition of various interference (melatonin, dopa, glycine, lysine, alanine, glucose, tryptophan, cystine, histidine etc.)

TEM was used to identify the size of synthesised nanoparticles shown in Fig. 1 (a) and the average particles size of La,N-CQD was 3–4 nm analysed by using ImageJ software and Fig. 1(b) shows histogram plots of average particles size. SEM image proves that the synthesised QDs were spherical and well dispersed revealed in Fig. 1(c), both SEM and TEM images favours the spherical size.

Additionally, XRD spectra shown in Fig. 1(d) confirms that synthesised QDs were amorphous in nature and diffraction angle 2θ was found to be 23° [56]. This is demonstrated by the graphite (002) plane. The FT-IR spectra of La,N-CQDs were shown in Fig. 1(e) demonstrating that the absorption at 1645 cm−1 suggested that CN bending was present and the N–H and O–H stretching vibration band found at 3300 cm−1. Further, the absorption in the range of 1250 cm−1 were attributed to C–N and C–O [57] and the stretching vibrations of carboxylic group (COO) can be seen at 1372 and 1560cm-1. The EDS spectrum shown in Fig. S1 proves successfully incorporation of lanthanum and nitrogen to carbon quantum dots. As seen in Fig. 1(f) the Zeta potential was evaluated in order to learn more about the characteristics of their surfaces, complex formation and electron density. The evaluated zeta potential values were −24 mV for La,N-CQD, indicating that the surfaces are negatively charged and electron rich species [58].

According to the X-ray photoelectron spectral (XPS) scan shown in Fig. 2 (a) La,N-CQDs was found to be primarily constituted of carbon, oxygen,nitrogen and lanthanum. O, N, C, La3d5/2 and La3d3/2 have binding energy maxima at 532eV, 398.7eV, 289.7eV, 833.2eV, and 858.6eV respectively. The high resolution spectrum of C1s shown in Fig. 2(b) shows two peaks at 284.3eVand 284.9eV that were due to CC and CO respectively. N1s High resolution spectrum shows two peaks at 397.2eV and 399.5eV that belongs to the C–N–C and N–H groups Fig. 2(c). Three peaks at 530.68eV, 532.8eV, and 53.2eV in the O1s spectrum are attributed to C–O–C, CO and La–O respectively Fig. 2(d). The peaks of La–H, LaO and La–O groups are apparent at 829eV, 833.6eV and 860.5eV respectively In the high resolved spectra of La3d5/2 and La3d3/2 as shown in Fig. 2(e) and (f)) [59].

UV absorption spectra of La,N-CQDs shows absorbance at 334 nm due to n-π* transitions shown in Fig. S2 [57]. Fluorescence studies of the La,N-CQDs were studied out in order to evaluate the optical characteristics. The color of aqueous solutions of La,N-CQDs was light green that emitted blue-green fluorescent on exposure to UV light. PL exhibited a sharp peak at 445 nm in the wavelength of fluorescence emissions. The absorption and emission spectra of synthesised La,N-CQDs shown in Fig. 3 (a).

The La,N-CQDs fluorescence emission spectra was also recorded using a variety of excitation wavelengths in range of 300 nm–400 nm that emerged excitation dependent in stages and the emission peak was centred between 420 and 460 nm (Fig. 3(b)). The maximum intensity was observed at excitation of 340 nm and reduction in intensity was observed with further increase in excitation. Effects of pH on fluorescence intensity (Fig. 3(c)) of La,N-CQDs was observed by using various buffer solutions ranging from 2.0 to 13.0 and maximum intensity was observed at pH 12 [58]. The quenching of intensity in acidic medium due to the protonation of carboxyl groups and aggregation of synthesised quantum dots [59,60]. Fig. 3(d) shows the histogram plots between various pH and fluorescence intensity. After constant exposure to UV radiation at 370 nm (150W Xenon lamp) in the spectrofluorometer, it was found that around 84 % of the initial fluorescence of La, N-CQDs was still present as shown in Fig. S3. The exceptional material stability of La,N-CQDs was observed at room temperature for emission over 30 days(Fig. S4).

The absorption spectra of various concentrations of Ser with La,N-CQDs was captured under ideal conditions, as shown in Fig. 4 (a). The absorption intensity of La,N-CQDs is found to decrease at 334 nm as Ser concentration raised. Similar to absorption spectra, the fluorescence intensity of La,N-CQDs also decreased (445 nm) as Ser concentration increased from 0 to 38 nM as shown in Fig. 4(c). The fluorescent colour shifts can be clearly seen with UV lamp upon addition of Ser. The calibration curves for the fluorescence ratio (F0/F) versus Ser concentrations are displayed in Fig. 4(d), the correlation coefficient value R2 is 0.9909 and the determined LOD for Ser was 7.4 nM. As shown in Table S1, the majority of methods for Ser detection at the moment are based on electrochemical detection. Only a few are reported based on optical methods thus we compared the proposed method to other reported methods and it was found that our methods are easy and more efficient to detect Ser than others. The uniqueness, high sensitivity and accuracy of the optical probe that was constructed are confirmed and this method's LOD is also highly competitive.

The potential fluorescence quenching process for CQDs sensing can be explained by a number of events including static quenching, inner filter effects, FRET, PET and dynamic quenching [41,42]. Using the time-correlated single-photon counting approach, the fluorescence life-time of La,N-CQDs was measured in the presence and absence of Ser in order to elucidate the potential quenching mechanism as shown in Table 1 . In the time-resolved fluorescence spectra, the fluorescence decay time of pure La,N-CQDs was observed as 10.38ns and on addition of Ser the life time was 10.19ns as shown in Fig. 5 (a). Since the lifetime is constant, the quenching mechanism might be a static process along with inner filter effect [61,62]. The development of the ground state complexes can affect the absorption spectra in case of static quenching. After adding Ser to La,N-CQDs, the UV absorption curve exhibited significant changes as shown in Fig. 4(a) demonstrating a non-luminous ground state complex formed by Ser and La,N-CQDs. We have examined the fluorescence spectra of La,N-CQDs with and without addition of serotonin to learn more about sensing mechanism (Fig. 4(c)) we found the fluorescence intensity decrease continuously with addition of Ser. According to the FT-IR spectra of synthesised QDs shows more oxygen containing groups on their surfaces, including C–O, CO and O–H groups. This allowed La,N-CQD to interact noncovalently with CO, phenyl, and amine functional groups in Ser through electrostatic, stacking and hydrogen bonding interactions.

Initial zeta potential value of La,N-CQDs was −24mV proving the negative charge on La,N-CQDs and that's increased to -9mV with addition of Ser (35 μM) signifying a strong electrostatic interaction between La,N-CQDs and Ser. These findings suggest that Ser would electrostatically adsorb onto La,N-CQDs, leading to static quenching for Ser detection [62,63]. The alteration in charge distribution and energy leads to fluorescence quenching of La,N-CQDs. Therefore, the generation of non-luminescent ground state complex causes the quenching mechanism of La,N-CQDs by Ser. We found the good spectral overlap between the absorption spectra of Ser and excitation spectra of La,N-CQDs shown in Fig. 4(b) that proves the mechanism of Inner filter effect [64]. It appears that both IFE and static quenching has reduced the fluorescent intensity of La,N-CQDs. As a result, taking into account the aforementioned discussion and the fluorescence quenching mechanism was produced due to IFE and static quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone application for detection of Ser
ce:section.ce:para: We can detect Ser using a smartphone as La,N-CQDs exhibits continuous colour variation from blue to brown in UV light with increase in concentration of Ser from 0 to 60 μM. Using an iPhone, pictures were taken after every addition of Ser to La,N-CQDs solution in the UV chamber. The B channel and R channel data (RGB colour model) of photographs were rapidly collected using a smartphone (iPhone) and colour scanning software (color detector). In order to assess the intensities of pictures absorption, the value of channel R/B was computed [65]. A portable smart phone can be used to perform semi-quantitative Ser detection, as shown in Fig. 5(b), where the R channel/B channel has an excellent percentage correlation (R2 = 0.9914) and the LOD is low as 13 μM.

An invisible luminous ink is used in information anticounterfeiting due to the fluorescence stability of La,N-CQDs. The word “NIT.J″ was written on two sheets of paper using the La,N-CQDs fluorescent ink that are not visible in visible light Fig. 6 (a) but the blue fluorescent was observed in UV light and the word NIT.J was clearly visible as shown in Fig. 6(b). It suggests that La,N-CQDs can be applied for information anti-counterfeiting as a sort of invisible ink. The written sheets were then spurted with ultrapure water and a Ser solution. The papers were dried and then pictures was taken using a 365 nm UV lamp. Fig. 6(c) on R.H.S demonstrates the diffusion action of water causes the disappearance of words on fine filter paper whereas L.H.S shows that fluorescent intensity decreased after addition of Ser but the words became apparent again. This is due to the fact that La,N-CQD and Ser quickly interacted to create non-radiative complexes. This not only demonstrates the employment of La,N-CQDs in paper encryption and anticounterfeiting but also supports the fluorescent quenching mechanism of La,N- CQDs with Ser.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Ser on colorimetric test papers
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent colorimetric test papers which has the benefits of being affordable, easy to use, and mobility, is used to visually observe the change in intensity and colour of the test papers under UV light in order to ascertain the presence and contents of the target analytes. In this study, a fluorescent colorimetric test papers based on La,N-CQDs was developed for the detection of Ser, 10 μL of the La,N-CQD working solution were poured onto the test papers and dried. Then 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60,70 μL of Ser solution dropped onto the above colorimetric paper developed and observed the changes under UV light shown in Fig. S5. We found that initial in absence of Ser pure La,N-CQD was dark and when different conc. of Ser added color of the La,N-CQD becomes lighter down that also explain the quenching mechanism.

For studying the selectivity behaviour of La,N-CQDs towards Ser, we have done similar quenching experiment for other species (tryptophan, dopa,glycine, lysine,melatonin, epinephrine,cystine, phenylalnaine) Fig. 7 (a) shows the quenching efficiency of all other species and it has been observed that their PL quenching is weaker than Ser. Hence the synthesised materials are highly selective for Ser. The synthesised quantum dots are also superior to detect Ser in the presence of other species in aqueous solution as is illustrated in Fig. 7(b).

The recognition of Ser in 1 % human urine samples was examined to show the biosensors capacity for sensing in real samples. Different final Ser concentrations (5,10,15and 20 nM) were spiked into samples of 1 % human urine. All samples were dilute 100 times with 0.1 M PBS (pH 12.0) and the diagnostic results were shown in Table 2 . The recoveries of Ser for determination in urine samples ranged from 98 % to 104 %. These findings demonstrated the brilliant specificity of the La,N-CQDs for the detection of Ser in complicated biofluid samples, indicating the technique's significant potential for biomedical applications.

In summary, a straightforward hydrothermal approach has been used for successfully production of La–N-CQDs having favourable features that detect Ser simultaneously and selectively with low limit of detection. Moreover, the La,N-CQDs showed blue fluorescence at 445 nm when excited at 340 nm. The synthesised QDs have excellent stability, good water solubility, high fluorescence and biocompatibility. The La,N-CQDs based nanosensor has good analytical performance with high selectivity and sensitivity towards Ser detection in an effective linear range of concentration 0–38 μM and low limit of detection 7.4 nM. The decrease in intensity of quantum dots was observed with addition of Ser and the life time is constant. A good spectral overlay between the absorption spectra of Ser and excitation spectra of La,N-CQDs has been observed. It is concluded that the mechanism is static quenching along with inner filter effect is going on owing to interactions between QDs and Ser. Additionally, it should be emphasized that our results significantly outperform those in the literature in terms of limit of detection, easy to use, portable high photostability, selectivity and sensitivity for detection of Ser. The suggested approach offered good stability, less expensive, simple, fast and easy to use. It can be utilised for information anti-counterfeiting ink as well as Ser detection mechanism proved through colorimetric test papers. A portable smartphone sensor was also developed for the sensing of Ser using La,N-CQDs. Additionally, it demonstrated outstanding recovery and accuracy while detecting Ser in spiked actual samples having good recoveries ranging from 98 % to 104 %, were obtained in real samples.
Jyoti Duhan: Conceptualization, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Sangeeta Obrai: Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The author declare no competing interest

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: I want to express my gratitude to the Central Research Facility at NIT Jalandhar as well as the Ministry of Human Resource and Development (MHRD) of India for the financial support.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.optmat.2023.114466.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effects of long-term light, darkness and oral administration of melatonin on serum levels of melatonin
